m365-347.attackPerspective: Hybrid admin accounts (synced from on-prem AD) are high-value targets because compromising the on-prem domain gives attackers a path to Entra ID global admin. Cloud-only accounts eliminate this lateral movement path. Attackers routinely target AD Connect servers to harvest synced credentials.
m365-347.tuningNotes: Service accounts synced from AD that hold directory roles are also a risk. Consider migrating them to Managed Identities or cloud-only service principals. Exclude break-glass accounts from Conditional Access but ensure they are cloud-only.
m365-347.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Detect sign-ins from synced admin accounts
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where UserType == "Member"
| join kind=inner (
    IdentityInfo | where OnPremisesDistinguishedName != ""
    | project AccountUPN, OnPremisesDistinguishedName
) on $left.UserPrincipalName == $right.AccountUPN
| join kind=inner (
    IdentityDirectoryEvents
    | where ActionType == "Role member added"
) on AccountUPN
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, AppDisplayName, IPAddress, Location

m365-348.attackPerspective: Without emergency access accounts, an organization can be permanently locked out of their tenant if MFA provider fails, Conditional Access misconfiguration blocks all admins, or during a federated IdP outage. Attackers also specifically look for break-glass accounts that lack proper monitoring — they're often excluded from CA policies, making them ideal persistence targets.
m365-348.tuningNotes: Store credentials in two separate physical safes. Use FIDO2 keys (not phone MFA) as the second factor. Test break-glass sign-in quarterly. Set up an Azure Monitor alert on any sign-in to these accounts — any use outside a declared emergency is a critical incident.
m365-348.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Alert on ANY sign-in to break-glass accounts
let breakGlass = dynamic(['breakglass1@yourdomain.onmicrosoft.com','breakglass2@yourdomain.onmicrosoft.com']);
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where UserPrincipalName in~ (breakGlass)
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, AppDisplayName, IPAddress, Location, ResultType, DeviceDetail
| extend AlertSeverity = 'Critical'

m365-349.attackPerspective: Each Global Admin account is an attack surface. More than 4 increases the blast radius of credential theft. Fewer than 2 creates a single point of failure. Attackers enumerate Global Admins via Microsoft Graph API reconnaissance to identify high-value targets for spear-phishing.
m365-349.implementationNotes:
# Count current Global Admins
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'RoleManagement.Read.Directory','User.Read.All'
$gaRole = Get-MgDirectoryRole -Filter "displayName eq 'Global Administrator'"
$members = Get-MgDirectoryRoleMember -DirectoryRoleId $gaRole.Id
Write-Host "Current Global Admins: $($members.Count)"
$members | ForEach-Object {
  $u = Get-MgUser -UserId $_.Id -Property displayName,userPrincipalName,onPremisesSyncEnabled
  [PSCustomObject]@{
    Name = $u.DisplayName
    UPN = $u.UserPrincipalName
    Synced = $u.OnPremisesSyncEnabled
  }
} | Format-Table

# Downgrade excess admins to least-privilege roles
# Example: Move to User Administrator or Security Administrator
# Remove-MgDirectoryRoleMember -DirectoryRoleId $gaRole.Id -DirectoryObjectId <userId>
m365-349.tuningNotes: The 2-4 count excludes break-glass accounts. In larger orgs, use PIM (Privileged Identity Management) to make GA assignments time-bound and require justification. Active GA assignments should still be 2-4; eligible assignments can be higher.
m365-349.advancedDetection:
// KQL: Track Global Admin role changes
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where OperationName in ('Add member to role','Remove member from role')
| where TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties[1].newValue has 'Global Administrator'
| project TimeGenerated, InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName, OperationName, TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName
| sort by TimeGenerated desc
